en.extract:
The sooty oystercatcher (Haematopus fuliginosus) is a species of oystercatcher. It is a wading bird endemic to Australia and commonly found on its coastline. It prefers rocky coastlines, but will occasionally live in estuaries. All of its feathers are black. It has a red eye, eye ring and bill, and pink legs.


Taxonomy
John Gould described the sooty oystercatcher in 1845. Its species name is the Latin adjective fuliginosus, "sooty". Two subspecies are recognised, the nominate from the coastline of southern Australia and subspecies ophthalmicus from northern Australia. The southern subspecies is larger and heavier than the northern. The northern one, with a more yellowish eye ring, is found from the Kimberleys across the top of the country to Mackay in central Queensland. There is considerable overlap, as the southern subspecies has been found up to Cape York. Subspecies ophthalmicus has been thought distinctive enough to warrant species status and needs further investigation. Black redbill is a local name.


Description

Measuring 42 to 52 cm (16.5 to 20.5 in) long with a bill length of 5–8 cm (2–3 in), the sooty oystercatcher has all black plumage, with pink-red legs and scarlet or orange-red bill and eyes. The heaviest of all oystercatchers, the sooty oystercatcher weighs up to 980 g (2.16 lb), averaging around 819 g (1.806 lb), with females larger and heavier in both subspecies. Males have shorter, thicker bills and females have longer, thinner bills. The 19% average difference in length is the most marked of any oystercatcher species. Immature birds have grey-brown legs, a bill tipped with brown, a browner cast to their plumage, and brown eyes. The bill, eyes and legs become red by the second year.


Distribution and habitat
Preferring rocky shores, the sooty oystercatcher is endemic to Australia. There are an estimated 11,500 individuals, 4000 of the nominate race and 7500 of the northern race. It is common around the Tasmanian coastline and Bass Strait islands. However it is declared Rare in South Australia and Queensland, Near Threatened in Victoria and Endangered in New South Wales.


Feeding
The sooty oystercatcher almost always forages in the intertidal zone, for the two hours either side of low tide. A field study published in 2011 showed that prey items differed markedly between the sexes with only a 36% overlap. Females focussed on soft-bodied prey which they could swallow whole such as fish, crabs, bluebottle jellyfish and various worm-like creatures such as cunjevoi, while males preferred hard-shelled prey such as mussels (Mytilus planulatus), sea urchins, turban shells (Lunella undulata and Lunella torquata), and black periwinkle (Nerita atramentosa).


Breeding
A clutch of two to three eggs is laid in a crevice in rocks or small hollow or flat on the ground, often on an island or high place where parent birds can keep watch. Tapered oval in shape, the eggs are buff to beige with dark brown and lavender dots and splotches and measure 63 mm (2.5 in) long by 42 mm (1.7 in) wide.


References


External links
 Media related to Haematopus fuliginosus at Wikimedia Commons
 Data related to Haematopus fuliginosus at Wikispecies

The '''sooty oystercatcher''' ('''''Haematopus fuliginosus''''') is a species of [[oystercatcher]]. It is a wading bird [[endemism|endemic]] to [[Australia]] and commonly found on its [[coastline]]. It prefers rocky coastlines, but will occasionally live in estuaries. All of its feathers are black. It has a red eye, eye ring and bill, and pink legs.

==Taxonomy==
[[John Gould]] described the sooty oystercatcher in 1845. Its species name is the [[Latin]] adjective ''fuliginosus'', "sooty". Two subspecies are recognised, the nominate from the coastline of southern Australia and subspecies ''ophthalmicus'' from northern Australia. The southern subspecies is larger and heavier than the northern.<ref name=hansen>{{Cite journal | last1 = Hansen | first1 = B. D. | last2 = Minton | first2 = C. D. T. | last3 = Jessop | first3 = R. | last4 = Collins | first4 = P. | title = Biometrics, sexing criteria, age-structure and moult of Sooty Oystercatchers in south-eastern and north-western Australia | doi = 10.1071/MU07045 | journal = Emu | volume = 109 | pages = 25–33 | year = 2009 | issue = 1 | bibcode = 2009EmuAO.109...25H | s2cid = 83482876 }}</ref> The northern one, with a more yellowish eye ring, is found from the Kimberleys across the top of the country to Mackay in central Queensland. There is considerable overlap, as the southern subspecies has been found up to [[Cape York Peninsula|Cape York]].<ref name="shorebirds">{{cite book|author1=Geering, Andrew |author2=Agnew, Lindsay |author3=Harding, Sandra |title=Shorebirds of Australia|publisher=Csiro Publishing|location=Collingwood, Victoria|year=2007|isbn=978-0643103009|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Mug1l_45OzMC&pg=PT195}}</ref> Subspecies ''ophthalmicus'' has been thought distinctive enough to warrant species status and needs further investigation.<ref>{{cite book |title=Systematics and Taxonomy of Australian Birds|author1=Christidis, Les |author2=Boles, Walter E. |year=2008 |publisher=CSIRO Publishing |location=Canberra |isbn=978-0-643-06511-6 |page=131|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=klm159tsD-oC&q=haematopus+fuliginosus&pg=PA131}}</ref> Black redbill is a local name.<ref name="TSISO"/>

==Description==
[[File:Sooty oystercatcher94 96.ogv|thumb|right|[[Mooloolaba, Queensland|Mooloolaba]], Australia]]
[[File:Sooty Oystercatcher eggs.jpg|thumb|right|Sooty oystercatcher eggs in a nest on the south coast of [[New South Wales]]]]

Measuring {{convert|42|to|52|cm|in|round=0.5|abbr=on}} long with a bill length of {{convert|5|–|8|cm|in|round=0.5|abbr=on}}, the sooty oystercatcher has all black plumage,<ref name="TSISO">{{cite web|url=http://www.southcoastshorebirds.com.au/shorebird_downloads/factsheets/Sooty%20Oystercatcher%20fact%20sheet.pdf|title=Sooty Oystercatcher|author=South Coast Shorebird Recovery Program|work=Threatened Species Information|publisher=NSW National Parks and Wildlife Service (NPWS)|access-date=2 September 2012}}</ref> with pink-red legs and scarlet or orange-red bill and eyes.<ref name="shorebirds"/> The heaviest of all oystercatchers, the sooty oystercatcher weighs up to {{convert|980|g|lb|abbr=on}}, averaging around {{convert|819|g|lb|abbr=on}},<ref name = "CRC">{{cite book|title=CRC Handbook of Avian Body Masses|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=TcnOTPILlcEC&pg=PA98| author=Dunning, John B. Jr.|publisher=CRC Press |year=1992|page=98|isbn= 978-0-8493-4258-5}}</ref> with females larger and heavier in both subspecies.<ref name=hansen/> Males have shorter, thicker bills and females have longer, thinner bills. The 19% average difference in length is the most marked of any oystercatcher species.<ref name=aplin11>{{Cite journal | last1 = Aplin | first1 = Lucy Margaret | last2 = Cockburn | first2 = Andrew| doi = 10.1111/j.1442-9993.2011.02263.x | title = Ecological Selection and Sexual Dimorphism in the Sooty Oystercatcher, ''Haematopus fuliginosus'' | journal = Austral Ecology | volume = 37 | issue = 2 | pages = 248–57| year = 2012 | bibcode = 2012AusEc..37..248A }}</ref> Immature birds have grey-brown legs, a bill tipped with brown, a browner cast to their plumage,<ref name="TSISO"/> and brown eyes. The bill, eyes and legs become red by the second year.<ref name="shorebirds"/>

==Distribution and habitat==
Preferring rocky shores, the sooty oystercatcher is endemic to Australia. There are an estimated 11,500 individuals, 4000 of the nominate race and 7500 of the northern race.<ref name="shorebirds"/> It is common around the Tasmanian coastline and Bass Strait islands.<ref name="tas">{{cite web|url=http://www.parks.tas.gov.au/index.aspx?base=17065|title=Sooty Oystercatcher, ''Haematopus fuliginosus''|name-list-style=amp|year=2010|work=Nature & Conservation|publisher=Department of Primary Industries, Parks, Water and Environment, Tasmanian Government|access-date=2 September 2012}}</ref> However it is declared Rare in South Australia and Queensland, Near Threatened in Victoria and Endangered in New South Wales.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.environment.nsw.gov.au/resources/nature/schedules/SootyOystercatcher.pdf|title=Sooty Oystercatcher ''Haematopus fuliginosus'': Review of Current Information in NSW|author=NSW Scientific Committee|date=May 2008|access-date=2 September 2012|location=Hurstville, NSW}}</ref>

==Feeding==
The sooty oystercatcher almost always forages in the intertidal zone, for the two hours either side of low tide.<ref name=aplin11/> A field study published in 2011 showed that prey items differed markedly between the sexes with only a 36% overlap. Females focussed on soft-bodied prey which they could swallow whole such as fish, crabs, bluebottle jellyfish and various worm-like creatures such as [[Pyura stolonifera|cunjevoi]], while males preferred hard-shelled prey such as mussels (''[[Mytilus planulatus]]''), sea urchins, turban shells (''[[Lunella undulata]]'' and ''[[Lunella torquata]]''), and black periwinkle (''[[Nerita atramentosa]]'').<ref name=aplin11/>

==Breeding==
A clutch of two to three eggs is laid in a crevice in rocks or small hollow or flat on the ground, often on an island or high place where parent birds can keep watch. Tapered oval in shape, the eggs are buff to beige with dark brown and lavender dots and splotches and measure {{convert|63|mm|in|abbr=on}} long by {{convert|42|mm|in|abbr=on}} wide.<ref>* {{cite book
  | last = Beruldsen
  | first = Gordon
  | author-link = Gordon Beruldsen
  | year = 2003
  | title = Australian Birds: Their Nests and Eggs
  | publisher = self
  | location = Kenmore Hills, Queensland
  | page = 216
  | isbn = 0-646-42798-9
  }}</ref>
